text: You need to read up on or watch videos about how much of today's fashion industry (indeed, many other industries too) are controlled by a monopoly of venture capital backed companies. It's not controlled by private owners anymore. A good case study is J.crew. Although they are not a sustainable brand, they're a good example of how a family owned business could not grow on their own. They had reached the ceiling of what they could accomplish. The founder wanted to retire but the daughter could not afford to buy him out. So they brought in outside investors. Once that happened, it became all about increasing profits YOY and less about sustaining the growth they've achieved. VC don't want a plateau, they want an upwards trending chart of growth. Look at any VC backed company and you see the same or similar story.

text: The definitions for both are a bit vague in my opinion, but I always like to compare the two based on the speed in which they are actually produced. Historically, fashion companies produced 2-4 seasons per year. In my opinion, companies that produce between 4-6 seasons per year are more likely to be considered slow fashion. The brands that constantly have newness and produce new drops (not just replenishment) are always fast fashion. Zara famously creates 52 seasons per year, Shein is basically something new every day (they have algorithms, not designers). Gap probably has somewhere around 12.

text:
What’s your main criteria for ethical fashion, and what’s your style? I find some guides like Good on You to be a good jumping off point (or googling articles about ethical/sustainable fashion), but nobody rates with the exact same criteria I would use. 

There are also some basic steps to take that make a big difference - for example, some years ago I swore off polyester and acrylic. These fibers are terrible for the environment and are really just wearing plastic. Once you’ve not worn them for a while you’ll feel it immediately if you pick something up and it will feel quite gross to you. That said, sometimes poly is still necessary as part of certain performance fabrics, in which case recycled poly or nylon is still going to be a better choice. 

Another thing you could do is to swear off fast fashion. If a brand updates their offerings a ton of times per year, they probably aren’t ethical. This is a very easy rule of thumb! 

None of us are going to be able to be completely ethical in our fashion, but we can put our money where our mouths and values are, one step at a time. I’d also look into slow fashion and resources on shopping and styling your own closet, which can make a huge difference.

text:
Durability or quality are probably the words you're looking for. 

Ethics regarding clothing is such a complex thing that hits on so many different topics: environmental waste, micro plastics, workers rights, plagiarism and cultural appropriation, the sheer magnitude of consumerism, planned obsolescence, cutting corners with the reduction of seams or fabric choice just to shave a few more pennies from manufacturing cost, the list goes on. 

For what it's worth, I've shopped a variety of brands and buy a lot of GAP clothes because they fit my budget once the sales are applied. And I have a circle of moms that pass the traveling baby clothes around. What is not picked, gets donated to daycare, so kids have something to wear on oopsie days. They've held up for several kids. I think we  do what we can. Ethics isn't black and white. Don't let a draconic voice convince you that you're awful because your not doing this 100% crunchy granola. Every step is a good one. 

All traditional brands are getting faster due to the pressure from fast fashion. GAP is not shien. But anything you find in the mall probably is green washed at best. 

If you're looking for slightly more durable stuff that holds it's colors, Boden and Tea each make stuff that is a tier above gap. Boden, I think is the better of the two (you'll find the designs sewn on in appliques rather than print screen) and there's a very good second hand market for them.

text:
I wouldn't necessarily say that applies to very small clothing brands that have small local workforces. 


As an example a brand like Curvature Clothing isn't fast fashion. It's a small clothing brand sewing clothes mainly for locals, with a small local workforce. The owner/designer puts out new items in major collections...but also whenever she damn well wants in between. If she detects an unmet  need...for example if she thinks "oh... everyone has now bought those puff sleeve dresses that are everywhere in the shops and have no cardigans cut to accommodate them" she can have something on the market very quickly indeed if she's already got an appropriate pattern from a few years ago  and can find a textile supply she is happy with.


Fast fashion isn't just about how often items are added. It's a whole business model. It's about driving consumption through relentless release of new looks. It's about deliberately trying to make people forget wearing what they already own so they can just  buy more.


https://www.curvatureclothing.com.au/pages/about-us


PS...I am not vouching for Curvature Clothing's general ethics because I haven't done a full check of them because I haven't been poised to buy from them yet. I just find the brand interesting to watch because it is local...and not fast fashion.

text:
Yes! Or anything with a modifiable waist, stretchy fabrics, fit and flare dresses. It would be lovely if sustainable fashion designers made some clothes based on an average shape. 

As it stands, sustainable fashion designers mostly uses the same "model body" that fast fashion uses - tall, thin, no curves. Fast fashion uses this body since a. tailoring is expensive and b. much of fast fashion caters to teenagers that have modest budgets and care about being "on-trend". This population has not grown into their adult bodies yet, they have a lot less body fat. 

With sustainable fashion, I am expecting pieces I can wear for a long time. I sure as heck won't buy anything where it is clear the designer has never gotten input from an actual regular-sized woman.

Also (IMPORTANT BECAUSE MANY FASHION DESIGNERS DON'T SEEM TO GET THAT), when I say curvy, I don't necessarily mean "overweight" (though there is still way too little in that space too), I mean "random regular-weight adult woman with boobs and a butt". Just walk out in pubic and watch what regular people's bodies actually look like ffs. 

Also, some colorful clothes. I can't stand all the beige/pale green/black. Give me some cerulean, wine red, fuchsia, cyan blue, grass green. No crazy patterned statement pieces necessary, a nicely proportioned unicolor dress is perfect.

text:
The easiest way is a marketplace that curates and researches brands for you:

* [Remake](https://remake.world/sustainable-brands/) (women's clothes)
* [Eco-Stylist](https://www.eco-stylist.com/) (men's clothes)
* [GoodonYou](https://goodonyou.eco/) (rates all brands, good & bad)
* [DoneGood](https://donegood.co/) (like the Amazon of social good)
* [Project Cece](https://www.projectcece.co.uk/) (marketplace for men + women's clothes)

These are a few sources I trust. 

In full disclosure I run Eco-Stylist and we partner with Remake to use their sustainable brand criteria to research every brand we partner with. They are a non-profit who's black and white framework looks at a brand holistically, giving points for living wages, worker wellbeing, sustainable fabrics, sustainable manufacturing, recycled packaging, saving water, not using chemicals, and more. 

GoodonYou is a source I trust because my own brand research usually matches up with theirs.

DoneGood does a brand survey for each brand they approve. I don't know what this survey looks like but generally agree with the brands they choose. 

Project Cece is Europe focused and I don't know a lot about how they rank brands, but worth looking into.
label: recommendation

text:
Your commitment to ethical fashion is really great! 

Look for thrift stores located in or near upscale or trendy neighborhoods near you. The bigger and busier the store the more variety and the clothes rotate through faster. 

Try searching for consignment stores too, those tend to have a boutique feel and you don’t have to dig through mountains of clothes. 

Poshmark is a good online site to buy secondhand but you will want to consider the environmental footprint of shipping (my rule is to only buy if I can find a couple items from the seller I wish to purchase). You also will want to have somewhat of an idea of how a particular brand or cut of clothing fits you. The other trick is you can google to get the brand’s size chart which helps but even so, sometimes the item just isn’t what you expected when you try it on.

I will shop at ethical fashion brands when they do clearance events, but even so, I try to limit new purchases because it’s just so expensive. But those brands are often really well constructed so if you get some good basics they will last for years, and basics don’t really fall out of fashion. 

Good luck with your ethical fashion journey!
label: recommendation

text:
Ethically made men’s business casual has been tough to track down in my experience. You might want to check out Asket (they attempt to track their supply chain) and 3Sixteen (made in USA):

https://www.asket.com/shop/trousers/chino-beige

https://www.3sixteen.com/collections/bottoms

There are a few brands that started out real hot on made in USA like Taylor Stitch and Ministry of Supply which have since moved their manufacturing abroad but still make some claims about oversight. Everlane makes great men’s chinos but not made in USA so up to you whether you’re on board with their system.

Secondhand has been the best (though exhausting) option for my household. I can’t wait until there’s a real, high volume thredUP equivalent for men.

Good luck!

text:
Hey, not quite the replacement you're looking for, but my solution has been to thrift/buy secondhand.

Ideally, we could live in a world where we wouldn't wear F21/H&M/Zara at all. By no means do I want to directly support these chains, but secondhand items are already there. It fares better for the environment if we were to make use of these items rather than create more clothing, ethically or not. 

I cycle through a bunch of fashion apps on top of physically visiting thrift stores to stay trendy. The four apps I use are Mercari, Depop, ThredUp, and Poshmark. I would recommend Mercari the most, since the market is huuuuge, and you can bargain with the sellers. The search functions make it very easy to find specific items too, in this case "brandy melville black crop top" would yield a ton of results, and you can almost always find a listing that is brand new or worn once, if you care about that kind of thing.
label: recommendation

text:
Here's an article worth reading: [https://atmos.earth/ethical-consumerism/](https://atmos.earth/ethical-consumerism/)

It's called The Twilight of the Ethical Consumer by Elizabeth Cline, a journalist who has written two books about shopping ethically. Here's her take: it's all well and good to buy ethical clothes, but ultimately, those actions are nil without serious policy work. We need to pressure companies to do better to improve working conditions, reduce environmental pollutants, and hold them accountable for corporate injustice. Making noise on social media is a good way to do this.

You should check out the #PayUp campaign on social media to see how consumer action (pressuring H&M and other companies to repay stolen wages during COVID-19) has helped retrieve wages for workers.

[https://payupfashion.com/](https://payupfashion.com/)

I found it helpful to merge the "personal" actions I take (e.g. shopping less, supporting ethical businesses) with political / policy-directed actions (e.g. signing petitions, aligning myself with campaigns like the Clean Clothes Campaign, Fashion Revolution, Remake Our World, and more). 

And to answer your question, I was changed after watching the film "The True Cost" :)
label: recommendation

text:
Well firstly, let go of the idea that you need to redo your entire wardrobe at once. This will almost certainly result in buying stuff that you only wear for a minute. 

The second thing is that at your price point, secondhand is the way to go. Buy vintage or thrifted shirts and crop them yourself with a sewing machine or tailors tape. You’ll learn a new skill and get to wear something you made yourself! 

Finally, yes, there is such thing as ethical jeans but not necessarily in the way you think. The fact of the matter is that all denim production uses vast amounts of water to grow the cotton and wash and fade the finished fabric or pair. With that in mind, if you want to buy new instead of secondhand, make sure you only buy one pair. Make sure they’re raw. If you can find jeans made with hemp instead of cotton, that’s a great way to cut back on the water consumption. I recommend going with a local brand, or a Japanese brand that makes small batch stuff. Buying natural indigo instead of synthetic will ensure that it’s made to higher environmental standards. Momotaro is an amazing brand for this. Imogene and Willie in the USA is also great for raw denim with really nice cuts. 

Let me know if you need further info!
label: recommendation

text:
Ciao I am Giulia, an italian fashion designer.

For my first project I have decided to focus on the most basic garment of all: the white t-shirt.

I have interpreted and designed eleventh models of white t-shirts, each one with a name and personality: Querida, Cassandra, Olivia, Arya, Odilia, Eos, Neda, Filomena, Melia, Raissa and Ginevra.

It is not all.

I really needed to create something meaningful and with a positive impact, both for the environment and for the society.

This is why from the environmental point of view, i have decided to use only natural fibers for the production of t-shirts and labels.

Eight t-shirt variants are 100% leftover stock cotton, while the other three variants are made with organic cotton GOTS certified (95% organic cotton, 5% spandex) and every t-shirt is designed and entirely made in Italy.

I have also found the way to create recycled paper product tags and biodegradable packaging.

If you want to have a look at my t-shirts you can find them in the following link:

[https://urike.it/](https://urike.it/)

Thank you!

text:
Last week I posted here about a [mailing list](https://gethonest.co) for ethical brands. There are way more companies than I can feature in an email list, so I decided to build a chrome extension to feature these brands in new tabs (in a beautiful, non-intrusive way)!

I’m experimenting with 2 new ideas and would love your feedback on which one is more useful.

1. During an amazon checkout, overlay ethical alternatives. For example, if you're shopping for a Calvin Klein T-shirt, suggest a similar Pact shirt instead ([https://wearpact.com](https://wearpact.com)).
2. A credit system for ethical stores.  Every day you have the extension installed you gain credits, which can be spent at ethical retailers. This one is a bit more complicated, since it requires a payment system and probably partnering with certain retailers, but I think it’s definitely possible!

So what do you think? Do either of these features sound useful? Which one should I spend time on?

You can download the extension here: [https://chrome.google.com/webstore/detail/honest/penabcampeckofjaopgibdjoickapnoj](https://chrome.google.com/webstore/detail/honest/penabcampeckofjaopgibdjoickapnoj)

I'm willing to pay more for ethically produced clothing because I recognise the value and time put into it - I sew myself amongst other fibrecrafts. What's most important to me is no plastics or synthetic materials. Things like wool are great for the environment, especially because sheep can be so useful in rewilding and habitat conservation programmes, and natural fabrics don't set off my sensory issues like synthetics do.

When buying brand new clothing, what's important to me is that it's a piece that will last at least many years of good use, with sturdy construction and quality materials with transparent sourcing.
